did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning networks (specifically CNNs like AlexNet, ResNet50, VGG16, InceptionV3, Xception) and machine learning for melanoma lesion detection and classification. It evaluates transfer learning performance and proposes a Cloud-Fog-Edge architecture for continuous retraining of these models to identify melanoma from non-melanoma lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is published in the Proceedings of the 7th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Control Systems (ICICCS-2023). It presents original work including a 'Proposed Work' section detailing a Cloud-Fog-Edge Computing hybrid architecture for melanoma detection, discusses specific deep learning models (Xception, U-net, FCN), and outlines a methodology for data preprocessing, model training, and evaluation. Although it contains a literature review, its primary purpose is to propose and describe a new technical system and methodology rather than solely summarizing prior literature.

title: Identification of Melanoma Lesions using Deep Learning Networks and Cloud Computing
authors: Palanki Nithya Sri Purna Anjaneya Kumar
M otepalli Sasi Sai M anoj
journal: Proceedings of the 7th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Control Systems (ICICCS-2023)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of early melanoma detection by proposing a Cloud-Fog-Edge hybrid computing architecture to facilitate continuous retraining of deep learning models. The authors evaluate the reliability of transfer learning using various CNN architectures, including AlexNet, ResNet50, VGG16, InceptionV3, and Xception, noting that minor dataset changes can significantly impact classifier performance. Experimental results indicate that AlexNet is the most reliable network among those tested, and the proposed system achieved an accuracy of 95.2% with a processing time of 0.374ms. The distributed architecture was found to reduce processing time by up to 76% compared to existing methods, thereby supporting real-time diagnosis and reducing latency. The work concludes that while transfer learning is popular, maintaining peak performance requires regular retraining cycles supported by adaptable system designs.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target for detection and classification in the proposed deep learning system.
Used as the negative class in the binary classification task to distinguish from melanoma.

all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on binary classification to identify melanoma lesions from non-melanoma lesions. It evaluates transfer learning using CNNs (AlexNet, ResNet50, VGG16, InceptionV3, Xception) for this classification task, reporting accuracy metrics (95.2% for the proposed work) and discussing precision, recall, and F1 scores in the context of binary classification.

datasets_used.description: The paper does not explicitly name the specific dataset, cohort, or image collection used for the experiments. It mentions using 'ImageNet' in the keywords, likely for pre-training weights in transfer learning, but does not specify the primary melanoma dataset (e.g., ISIC, HAM10000) used for the classification task. The study focuses on a proposed Cloud-Fog-Edge architecture and evaluates model performance without detailing the source data statistics.

use.training: Used for training deep learning models (AlexNet, ResNet50, VGG16, InceptionV3, Xception) via transfer learning.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the proposed system's accuracy (95.2%) and processing time (0.374ms).
use.other: ImageNet is mentioned in keywords, likely used for pre-trained weights.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It relies on descriptive metrics such as accuracy (95.2%), processing time, and comparative performance of different deep learning architectures without statistical significance testing.

Data improvement (mentioned in conclusion as not being used, i.e., 'without segmentation and data improvement')
Segmentation (mentioned in conclusion as not being used, i.e., 'without segmentation')
conclusions: The integration of computer vision, machine learning, and deep learning is important for the early identification and treatment of melanoma, but experienced clinicians must remain involved in the decision-making process.

Transfer learning reliability is sensitive to dataset changes; even minor changes to dataset parameters can significantly affect classifier performance, necessitating regular training and testing cycles.

A proposed Cloud-Fog-Edge hybrid computing architecture for melanoma detection reduces the running time of continuous retraining by up to 76% compared to existing methods, thereby supporting real-time diagnosis and reducing latency.
Among the tested deep learning architectures (AlexNet, ResNet50, VGG16, InceptionV3, and Xception), AlexNet was identified as the most reliable network for transfer learning in this context.
The proposed system achieved an accuracy of 95.2% with a processing time of 0.374ms.

Deep learning algorithms can achieve high levels of performance without the need for elaborate preprocessing steps such as normalization and augmentation, although data preprocessing remains essential for ensuring data quality.

While Xception performed better than ResNet50, VGG16, and InceptionV3 in literature comparisons, the study's experimental results highlighted AlexNet's superior reliability for the specific task of melanoma detection under varying dataset conditions.
The system design must be adaptable to changes in training datasets while minimizing the running time of continuous retraining to maintain peak performance.
important_remarks: The specific dataset used for training and testing is not explicitly named or described in the text, limiting reproducibility and assessment of population bias.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; results rely on descriptive metrics only.

The paper claims AlexNet is the 'most reliable' network but does not provide comparative accuracy or statistical data for AlexNet versus other models (ResNet50, VGG16, InceptionV3, Xception) to substantiate this claim.
The study notes that transfer learning performance is sensitive to minor dataset changes, suggesting potential instability and lack of generalizability without continuous retraining.

The proposed system achieved 95.2% accuracy, but the lack of detailed dataset statistics (class balance, image resolution, source) makes it difficult to evaluate the clinical relevance or robustness of this metric.

The paper mentions that deep learning algorithms can achieve high performance without normalization or augmentation, which contradicts common best practices and may indicate overfitting or data leakage if not rigorously validated.
The work is a conference paper with limited scope, focusing on system architecture (Cloud-Fog-Edge) and processing time reduction (76%) rather than comprehensive clinical validation.
The authors emphasize that experienced clinicians must remain involved in decision-making, acknowledging the limitations of AI as an assistive tool rather than a replacement.
